paper_title: Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization: Teacher in Prompts, Not Gradients

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Knowledge distillation transfers a teacher's competence to a small student but is brittle in the small-student regime: forcing the student to imitate logits from a much larger teacher concentrates it on the teacher's sharpest modes, hurting generalization on benchmark families beyond the training corpus. Reinforcement learning (RL) avoids logit imitation by training on the student's own rollouts. However, on questions where every rollout fails-yielding zero advantage and being silently discarded-injecting a stronger teacher's response into the policy gradient breaks the on-policy assumption and induces drift. We introduce Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization (ZPPO), inspired by Vygotsky's zone of proximal development, which keeps the teacher inside the prompt rather than the policy gradient. On hard questions, ZPPO constructs two reformulated prompts: a Binary Candidate-included Question (BCQ) pairs one correct teacher response with one incorrect student response as anonymized candidates the student must discriminate, and a Negative Candidate-included Question (NCQ) aggregates the student's wrong rollouts into a single prompt to surface their shared failure modes. A prompt replay buffer recirculates each hard question until it either graduates-the student's mean rollout accuracy on it reaches half- or is FIFO-evicted under finite capacity, amplifying BCQ and NCQ inside the student's current zone of proximal development. On the Qwen3.5 family at four student scales (0.8B-9B) with a 27B teacher, post-trained as vision-language models and evaluated on a 31-benchmark suite (16 VLM, 10 LLM, 5 Video), ZPPO outperforms off/on-policy distillation and GRPO, with the largest gains at the smallest scale.

sections.content: Knowledge distillation transfers teachers competence to small student but is brittle in the small-student regime: forcing the student to imitate logits from much larger teacher concentrates it on the teachers sharpest modes, hurting generalization on benchmark families beyond the training corpus. Reinforcement learning (RL) avoids logit imitation by training on the students own rollouts. However, on questions where every rollout failsyielding zero advantage and being silently discardedinjecting stronger teachers response into the policy gradient breaks the on-policy assumption and induces drift. We introduce Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization (ZPPO), inspired by Vygotskys zone of proximal development, which keeps the teacher inside the prompt rather than the policy gradient. On hard questions, ZPPO constructs two reformulated prompts: Binary Candidate-included Question (BCQ) pairs one correct teacher response with one incorrect student response as anonymized candidates the student must discriminate, and Negative Candidate-included Question (NCQ) aggregates the students wrong rollouts into single prompt to surface their shared failure modes. prompt replay buffer recirculates each hard question until it either graduates the students mean rollout accuracy on it reaches half or is FIFO-evicted under finite capacity, amplifying BCQ and NCQ inside the students current zone of proximal development. On the Qwen3.5 family at four student scales (0.8B9B) with 27B teacher, post-trained as visionlanguage models and evaluated on 31-benchmark suite (16 VLM, 10 LLM, 5 Video), ZPPO outperforms off/on-policy distillation and GRPO, with the largest gains at the smallest scale. Our project page is available at link. Project Lead, byungkwanl@nvidia.com 2026 NVIDIA. All rights reserved. Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization Figure 2 (a) Two failure modes that motivate ZPPO. In the small-student regime, fitting the student to much larger teachers logits is brittle; on the RL side, injecting teacher response into the policy gradient as if it were on-policy introduces drift. (b) BCQ recovers learning signal on hard questions by pairing correct teacher response with wrong student response as anonymized in-prompt candidates, asking the student to reason about which one is correct. (c) NCQ aggregates the students own wrong rollouts into single prompt, surfacing shared error patterns. 1. Introduction Large language models (LLMs) and visionlanguage models (VLMs) have made rapid progress [15] on broad benchmarks, with reinforcement learning post-training [510]. The cost of those gains, however, has scaled with the cost of the model: tens of billions of activated parameters. Nearly all of the gains we see at the frontier do not transport to the regime that matters for deployment on mobile phones, AR/VR glasses, or embodied robots [1116], where compute and memory budgets are much smaller. Closing this deployment gap requires small models that generalize broadly, not narrow specialists. The standard knowledge-transfer recipe is knowledge distillation: small student is trained to imitate the logits, hidden states, or sampled outputs of strong teacher [1721]. Despite its effectiveness, distillation carries structural limitation that becomes severe when the student is much smaller than the teacher. Logit imitation is, at its core, supervised fitting on the teachers distribution: with limited training data, students memorize teacher answers [2224], and the limited capacity (e.g., mode-seeking bias) concentrates the student on the teachers sharpest peaks [20, 25]. The result is student whose generalization on benchmark families beyond the training corpus is brittle, and whose responses rarely venture beyond the paths the teacher has already taken (Tab. 2). line of work tries to alleviate these issues by using the students own rollouts to conduct distillation from the teacher to the student (on-policy distillation [2531]) or by removing the external teacher and letting the student teach itself (self -distillation [3234, 22, 35]). These reduce the teacherstudent capacity gap, but they all share the same fundamental constraint: the training signal is still logit distribution that the student must match. For 0.8B or 2B students, model capacity is too limited to absorb such distribution broadly, and the resulting models lose generalization on benchmarks beyond the narrow training data [2224]. natural alternative is reinforcement learning (RL) [5, 36, 37, 8, 7, 9, 10, 3841], which avoids logit imitation entirely and trains directly on the students own rollouts. However, RL has its own pathology in the small-model regime: hard questions with zero successful rollouts produce zero 2 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization Figure 3 Overview of ZPPO. (a) Hard questions (mean rollout accuracy below half) are admitted to the prompt replay buffer. (b) BCQ pairs one correct teacher response with one wrong student response. (c) NCQ aggregates the students wrong rollouts into single prompt. (d) Integrated batch drives the policy gradient update with RL Recipe. advantage and are silently discarded, so the student never learns from the questions where it most needs help. simple fix using stronger teachers correct response as if it were the students breaks the on-policy assumption and induces policy drift, because the teachers response lies far outside the students rollout distribution. Fig. 2a summarizes these two failure modes. In this paper, we ask single question: how can we transfer the teachers knowledge to the student without ever imitating the teachers logits or injecting the teachers response directly into the students policy gradient? Our answer, which we call Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization (ZPPO), borrows its name from Vygotskys zone of proximal development [42] the band of tasks learner cannot yet solve alone but can solve with small amount of guidance from more capable peer. Concretely, ZPPO keeps the teacher only inside the prompt, never inside the students policy gradient (Fig. 2b,c and Fig. 3): For each hard question (defined as one where the students mean rollout accuracy is below half), we construct reformulated prompt Binary Candidate-included Question (BCQ) that appends one correct teacher response and one wrong student response as shuffled, anonymized candidates, and asks the student to solve the problem afresh while consulting them. Since the student generates its own response, its policy gradient remains on-policy at the response level. In parallel, we construct Negative Candidate-included Question (NCQ) that appends all of the students wrong responses, explicitly shows the student that all of them are wrong, and asks for correct answer. Within independent rollout groups, rollouts cannot see each others wrong answers; inside our training loop, NCQ is the first place at which students failed attempts become collectively visible to the student, turning independent negatives into shared signal. Hard questions are also pushed into prompt replay buffer (no responses are stored). This focuses repeated practice precisely on prompts inside the students zone of proximal development. These three components (BCQ, NCQ, and prompt replay buffer) are layered on top of GRPO [36] augmented with DAPOs clip-higher and token-level policy gradient loss [8]. We also adopt two simple but easily mishandled recipe choices that materially affect small models training dynamics: iterations per step and batch-level advantage normalization of REINFORCE++ [37], with zeroadvantage groups excluded from the batch statistics. Both are low-cost, yet ablating them accounts for sizeable fraction of the performance drop we observe, despite their simplicity. We instantiate ZPPO on the Qwen3.5 family at four student scales 0.8B, 2B, 4B, and 9B with 27B teacher [4], post-train each student as VLM on multimodal RL dataset of imagequestion pairs, and evaluate on 31-benchmark suite spanning 16 VLM, 10 LLM, and 5 Video benchmarks. At every scale, ZPPO outperforms (i) offand on-policy distillation and (ii) GRPO  (Fig. 1)  , and ablating any single component BCQ, NCQ, or the prompt replay buffer strictly degrades the recipe at every scale (Tab. 3, Tab. 12). The improvements are largest precisely where the small-model gap is widest 0.8B student gains +9.3 pp on the VLM benchmarks, while 9B student gains +2.8 pp and, crucially, ZPPO also improves on the LLM and Video benchmark families that lie beyond the training corpus, the very families where distillation hurts. 4 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization Figure 4 Cumulative graduate counts (graduated / admitted = ratio) for ZPPO vs. GRPO at 2B by entry rollout accuracy at admission; denotes augmentation with the prompt replay buffer. sections.content: 2. Related Work Three lines of work bear on ZPPO. Knowledge distillationwhether off-policy, on-policy, or selfdistillation [18, 21, 25, 26, 43, 32]shares single training signal, logit distribution the student must match, which recent analyses show becomes brittle when the student is much smaller than the teacher, inducing memorization and mode-seeking [2224]. RL post-training with rule-based rewards on PPO and group-relative variants [44, 36, 8, 5] has advanced reasoning, but leaves the small-student failure mode where every rollout fails and the group advantage is zero [8] unaddressed; hybrid RL+distillation fixes [45, 46] target this mode, but route teacher responses into the students policy gradient through an auxiliary distillation term, which can re-introduce drift. Prompt replay, curriculum, and hint scaffolding [4752] re-prioritize or scaffold hard questions, but each path compromises different channel: prompt replay and curriculum [4749] re-expose the same hard prompt without adding any new signal about why the previous attempt failed; hint methods [52] append directional cue (without the answer) that the student tends to copy as shortcut without necessarily internalizing why the direction is correct; and prefix methods [50, 51] insert part of the teachers response as prefix into the students rollout: the student is trained on off-policy trajectories conditioned on teacher response. ZPPO differs from all three by moving teacher knowledge from the gradient to the prompt via two reformulations (BCQ and NCQ), so every response is sampled from the students own policy, with the prompt replay buffer amplifying inside the students zone of proximal development [42]. (see Appendix for full version) 3. Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization ZPPO is built on top of GRPO [36]. We first set up notation and identify the precise failure mode that motivates ZPPO (Sec. 3.1); we then describe the two prompt reformulations BCQ and NCQ that recover learning signal on hard questions (Sec. 3.2) and the prompt replay buffer that amplifies them (Sec. 3.3). How the reformulated rollouts plug into the training loop, together with two recipe-level choices on the backbone, is described at Sec. 4.1. The full ZPPO training step is summarized as Algorithm 1 in Appendix C. 5 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization 3.1. (1) is the textbook group-relative advantage that ZPPO conceptually builds on; the exact estimator used in our experiments is the two-step REINFORCE++ variant of Hu [37] (Step 1 subtracts the per-group mean; Step 2 batch-normalizes across the non-trivial groups), restated in our notation as Eqs. (2)(3) in Appendix C. The student update applies the PPO surrogate [44] on top of 𝐴(𝑔). Either form leaves blind spot for small students. Whenever rollout group is all-wrong (𝑟𝑥 = 0) or all-correct (𝑟𝑥 = 1), every advantage in the group is exactly zero, so the question contributes no gradient signal at all. For small student, the all-wrong case is exactly the set of questions that could be solved with teacher guidance. ZPPOs goal is to recover learning signal on those hard questions without ever placing teacher response in the students gradient. We call 𝑥 hard question when 𝑟𝑥 < 0.5 and use this single threshold throughout; the cutoff is not arbitrary, since under {0, 1} rewards std𝑥 is maximized at 𝑟𝑥 = 0.5, where the group-relative advantage carries the strongest learning signal [7]. 3.2. Prompt Reformulation: BCQ and NCQ 𝑔= }𝐺S Both BCQ and NCQ start from hard question 𝑥 on which we have already drawn 𝐺S student rollouts {𝑦(𝑔) . In parallel, we sample 𝐺T teacher rollouts on 𝑥 from frozen teacher policy 𝜋T, score them with the same outcome reward, and keep the correct ones as the pool {𝑦(+) } from which BCQ draws candidates. We use on-policy throughout in the response-level sense: every gradientcounted token is sampled from the current student. BCQ/NCQ prompts do contain teacher-derived text (correct and wrong candidate traces and, for NCQ, the parsed wrong-answer list), but this text is part of the input prompt and never enters the policy gradient as target. Because we re-sample teacher rollouts every time 𝑥 is seen whether new or replayed from ℬ the candidates that BCQ uses change on every visit. (i) Candidate compression: Before any candidate enters prompt, the frozen teacher rewrites it into short reasoning trace under shared compression prompt and shared token cap (Appendix D); the same prompt and cap are applied to teacher-correct and student-wrong traces. The rewritten text still appears only inside the prompt. (ii) Binary Candidate-included Question (BCQ): For each hard question that admits at least and one wrong student rollout 𝑦() one correct teacher response, BCQ uniformly samples one 𝑦(+) , teacher-compresses both responses, anonymizes them inside identical <candidate> tags, randomly shuffles the order, and appends the result to 𝑥 together with single instruction (the verbatim code-side template, including the per-candidate <candidate> blocks, is reproduced in Appendix D.1): Here are two candidate responses in <candidate> </candidate> tags to the question above. One is correct and another is wrong. The student then samples new rollout group {𝑦(𝑔) 𝑔=1 𝜋𝜃( 𝑥BCQ) from the reformulated prompt; every response token is generated by the current student, so the policy gradient remains BCQ}𝐺S 6 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization Figure 5 (a) Composition of single training rollout batch over the full run into Easy (𝑟𝑥 0.5), BCQ, and NCQ shares, per student scale. (b) Cumulative graduation vs. FIFO-eviction ratio of the prompt replay buffer. on-policy. The pedagogical effect comes from placing correct teacher response and wrong student response side by side anonymized, shuffled, and presented without correctness label so the student is trained to select and reason between the two candidates without any explicitly labeled target entering the gradient. (iii) Negative Candidate-included Question (NCQ): For each hard question, NCQ collects every wrong student rollout 𝑦() in the current group, parses out each rollouts final answer and lists the parsed answers explicitly inside the prompt, and appends each teacher-compressed reasoning trace as <candidate> block, with the instruction (verbatim code-side template in Appendix D.1): The following answers are all WRONG: parsed answer . Below are the incorrect reasoning processes in <candidate> </candidate> tags. NCQ}𝐺S The student then samples new rollout group {𝑦(𝑔) 𝑔=1 𝜋𝜃( 𝑥NCQ). As with BCQ, every 𝑦(𝑔) is entirely student-generated, so the policy gradient is on-policy at the response level. The NCQ pedagogical role of NCQ, however, is structurally different. In standard rollout group, each wrong rollout contributes its own advantage to the students policy gradient independently, and within independent rollout groups the student has no way to see patterns across its failures. Within our training loop, NCQ is the first place at which independently sampled wrong rollouts on the same question converge into single prompt: confronted with its own failed attempts, the student is cued to recognize the shared error patterns and avoid them. 3.3. Integration with Prompt Replay Buffer The prompt replay buffer ℬ exists solely to amplify BCQ and NCQ on questions the student has not yet mastered. It stores only the question 𝑥 (image and text), never any rollout responses. (i) Admission and graduation: After each training step we update ℬ from the current rollout batch: question 𝑥 is admitted if its 𝑟𝑥 < 0.5, and an already-admitted 𝑥 is graduated (removed) on any later step where 𝑟𝑥 reaches half (𝑟𝑥 0.5). Because BCQ and NCQ are constructed only on hard questions, every replayed question is eligible for one or both reformulations on its next visit. The buffer therefore always tracks the students current zone of proximal development. (ii) Sampling and capacity: Each rollout batch combines new questions from the data loader with replay samples drawn uniformly from ℬ, where the replay count is fixed fraction 𝜌replay of the new-question count. From the union of new and replayed questions, BCQ and NCQ are constructed on the hardest ones first (ranked by ascending 𝑟𝑥); the combined BCQ+NCQ count per rollout step is then capped at fraction 𝜌aug of the new-question count (Appendix C). (see Appendix for ZPPO algorithm) 4. Experiments 4.1. Setup Students and teacher. We instantiate ZPPO on Qwen3.5 [4] at four student scales (0.8B, 2B, 4B, 9B) with 27B teacher, post-training each student as VLM on the ZPPO-77K multimodal RL dataset (Appendix B). Training pipeline. ZPPO follows GRPO [36] with three DAPO ingredients [8]: clip-higher, token-level policy gradient loss, and removal of the KL penalty against reference policy. We also use 𝐼 = 4 iterations per step (vs. the standard 𝐼 = 16) and batch-level advantage normalization [37] with zero-advantage groups excluded from the batch statistics. Baselines. We compare ZPPO against four families from Sec. 2. (i) Off-policy distillation (Algorithm 2): imitate fresh teacher rollouts drawn online and filtered by correctness on every step. (ii) On-policy distillation (Algorithm 3): the student samples its own response, the teacher re-scores it for target logits, and the student imitates them. (iii) GRPO (Algorithm 4): ZPPOs pipeline without BCQ, NCQ, and the replay buffer. (iv) GRPO: ZPPOs pipeline without BCQ, NCQ ( marks prompt replay buffer). Details in Appendix D.2. 4.2. Main results Tab. 1 (16 VLM benchmarks) and Tab. 2 (10 LLM and 5 Video benchmarks) report the headline comparison; 4B/9B blocks are in Tab. 10 and Tab. 11. Benchmark details are in Appendix B.2. (i) Distillation hurts generalization beyond the training corpus. The best distillation variant stays within 1 pp of the original on VLM, while Offand On-Policy distillation lose accuracy on Video and degrade the LLMVideo average at every scale (Tab. 2, Tab. 11), sharpest at 0.8B. (ii) Replay alone is insufficient. Adding the buffer to either distillation or GRPO yields only marginal Avg gains in Tab. 1 (+0.5/+0.4/+1.6 pp at 0.8B for Off-Distill, On-Distill, GRPO): re-exposure without new learning signal does not recover the hard question loss. 8 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization Figure 6 RL recipe ablation at 2B on the 10 LLM (a, d), 16 VLM (b, e), and 5 Video (c, f) benchmarks. (Top, ac) Iterations per step 𝐼 {1, 4, 8, 16}. (Bottom, df) Batch-level advantage normalization: No norm, Norm w/o Zero (ZPPO default), and Norm w/ Zero. Dashed gray line marks original 2B. (iii) ZPPO improves the macro average at every scale and benchmark family. The Avg Δ row is positive across all blocks of Tab. 1 and Tab. 2, with the largest gains where the small-student gap is widest. The buffer-isolated ZPPOGRPO macro-average gain is robust to benchmark resampling, as quantified by the cluster bootstrap of Appendix F.6 (Tab. 14); this CI measures benchmark-selection robustness, not per-example or run-to-run uncertainty. Crucially, ZPPO improves generalization where distillation degrades it. 4.3. Inside the Zone: Admission to Graduation Fig. 4 reports cumulative graduations at 2B by entry rollout accuracy, and Fig. 5 reports batch composition and graduation across all four scales. (i) BCQ and NCQ provide usable learning signal on the hardest questions  (Fig. 4)  . On entries admitted at 0% rollout accuracy where ordinary RL has zero gradient signal ZPPO graduates 28% (432/1568) vs. GRPOs 4% (73/2035); on the next-hardest, 54% vs. 14%. The student absorbs the reformulated signals over training. (ii) Batch and graduation dynamics with scale  (Fig. 5)  . Fig. 5a shows pre-cap BCQ/NCQ eligibility (Algorithm 1). BCQ requires the teacher to succeed where the student fails; as students scale up, the remaining hard questions are increasingly ones the teacher also fails on, so BCQ candidates dry up (Fig. 5a), thinning the recovery signal. In the replay buffer, small scales mostly evict before graduating, large scales mostly graduate before eviction (Fig. 5b). ZPPOs gain is steepest where the zone of proximal development is widest. At 0.8B and 2B, hard questions persist long enough for BCQ and NCQ to revisit them repeatedly, compounding into the largest accumulated improvements; at 4B and 9B the same questions either graduate or rotate out before the reformulations can stack. Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization Figure 7 All-averaged gain Δ (pp) across teacher size 4.4. Component ablation Tab. 3 isolates each ZPPO component on the 16 VLM benchmarks (4B and 9B in Tab. 12). Starting from GRPO, we incrementally add the buffer (), both reformulations together (Both = BCQ + NCQ), and each reformulation paired with the buffer. (i) Each isolated component is modest. Replay alone (GRPO) and reformulation alone (GRPO + Both) move only small margin over GRPO at every scale useful, but not sufficient. (ii) Replay reformulation is super-additive. Pairing BCQ or NCQ with the buffer compounds far beyond the sum of isolated effects, because the buffer keeps re-exposing each hard question to fresh candidate set per visit. (iii) The full recipe is strongest at every scale. ZPPO ties or achieves the column max on every benchmark of Tab. 3 and Tab. 12. Within the recipe, BCQs contribution shrinks with scale and NCQs grows mirroring Fig. 5a so weaker students lean on the teacher candidate while stronger students lean on collective failure analysis. The same pattern holds for generalization (Appendix F.4, Tab. 13). 4.5. Additional RL recipe choices Beyond BCQ, NCQ, and the buffer, two seemingly minor recipe choices materially shape smallstudent RL. We isolate them at 2B across all three benchmark groups in Fig. 6. (i) Iterations per step trade off-policy drift against rollout cost; 𝐼 = 4 is the sweet spot. Larger 𝐼 grows in-step drift: each minibatch pushes the gradient further from the current policy. GRPOs default 𝐼 = 16 [36, 8] runs 4 as many updates as 𝐼 = 4 but yields only marginal gain over the original 2B base (Fig. 6ac); 𝐼 = 1 removes drift but undertrains at matched step count. 𝐼 = 4 pays small drift cost but performs four updates per step the trade that peaks on all three (Fig. 6ac). (ii) Excluding zero-advantage groups from batch normalization (Norm w/o Zero) is critical. Norm w/o Zero (ZPPO default) consistently beats No norm, while Norm w/ Zero clearly degrades below both (Fig. 6df). Both Norm variants share REINFORCE++ two-step form [37]; they differ only in whether zero-advantage groups are used (Appendix C, Eqs. 23). Trivial groups (all-correct/all-wrong) carry no learning signal, but their zero advantage values shrink the batch standard deviation that normalizes every other advantage, causing inflated advantages. 10 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization Qwen3.5-0.8B Qwen3.5-2B Method Base +GRPO +GRPO+Hint +GRPO+Prefix +GRPO+BCQ +ZPPO LLM VLM Video LLM VLM Video 25.2 28.7 28.8 27.1 31.5 33.1 41.0 45.4 47.2 45.5 48.6 50.3 48.3 50.5 50.0 49.1 52.0 52.8 45.3 47.3 47.6 46.3 49.1 50.4 56.8 59.2 60.4 59.5 60.8 62.0 60.6 61.9 61.6 61.0 62.6 63. Table 4 Comparison with Hint and Prefix methods 4.6. Discussion and Analysis (i) Teacher size matters: larger teacher widens ZPPOs gain  (Fig. 7)  ; shrinking the teacher to 4B/9B collapses the gain toward the GRPO+NCQ level, since BCQ requires the teacher to succeed where the student fails, and that pool of teacher-correct rollouts dries up as the teacher gets smaller. The drop is steepest at 0.8B, where ZPPOs gain is most BCQ-dominated (Sec. 4.4). (ii) Hint, Prefix vs. BCQ: We compare BCQ against two prompt-side teacher-guidance families: Hint appends an answer-free hint to the prompt; and Prefix forces teacher response prefix into the student and lets the student generate the rest (Appendix F.5). Tab. 4 shows that Hint (Algorithm 5) helps VLM but transfers less to the LLM/Video evaluation suite, since teacher hints act as directional shortcut without why the direction is correct; Prefix (Algorithm 6) transfers even less because the teacher prefix is off-policy, so drift accumulates over training. BCQ alone beats both because the student is trained to select between two anonymized candidates while reasoning, whereas Hint conditions the prompt on labeled teacher-derived hint and Prefix routes teacher-generated prefix into the response itself; full ZPPO opens the widest gap across all. (iii) Further evidence: buffer dynamics (App. F.1), ZPPO vs. GRPO dynamics (App. F.2), multimetric dynamics across scales (App. F.3), the component ablation on the LLM/Video benchmarks (App. F.4), Hint/Prefix setup (App. F.5), cluster-bootstrap CIs (App. F.6), BCQ and NCQ dynamics (App. F.7), teacher capability (App. G), and compute cost (Tab. 9) are deferred to the appendix. 5. Conclusion Distillation directly imitates teacher logits and teacher prefix-injection RL splices teacher tokens into the policy gradient, breaking the on-policy assumption and weakening generalization at small student scales. ZPPO answers our research question by keeping the teacher only inside the prompt, so the students gradient stays on its own rollouts while still benefiting from teacher knowledge. The flip side is structural ceiling: questions on which both teacher and student fail admit no BCQ candidate and leave only NCQ, whose contribution alone is limited the zone collapses to the NCQ-only regime. Extending the students zone beyond what the current teacher covers is, in our view, an important open problem this work leaves behind. 11 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Teacher-bounded zone. The most fundamental limitation of ZPPO is the one we identify in Sec. 5: BCQ requires the teacher to succeed on hard question, so questions on which both teacher and student fail admit no BCQ candidate and leave only NCQ, whose contribution alone is more limited (Tab. 3, Sec. 4.4). At the upper end of the student-capability range, BCQs contribution shrinks accordingly and the available zone effectively collapses to the NCQ-only regime. Extending the zone beyond what the current teacher covers via curated synthetic prompts, ensembles of teachers, or curriculum-aware question selection is, in our view, the most important open problem this work leaves behind. Tension with dynamic sampling. Dynamic sampling [8] simply deletes any question where the student got every rollout correct or every rollout wrong. On the other hand, ZPPO does the opposite: those all-wrong questions are exactly the ones we store in the replay buffer, so that BCQ and NCQ can rewrite the prompt and give the student fresh chance. Hence, naively combining ZPPO and dynamic sampling is not trivial. natural fix is to run them in sequence: first identify hard questions and only call dynamic sampling if their BCQand NCQ-reformulated rollouts still come back all-correct or all-wrong that is, apply dynamic sampling on top of BCQ and NCQ. We leave this hybrid algorithm to future work. Scope beyond reasoning correctness. ZPPO post-trains an already-built compact VLM and is orthogonal to how such small models are first constructed via architectural designs that compress visuallinguistic computation into small backbones [5357] or via large-to-small distillation of strong teachers [5860] so it could in principle be stacked on any of these as later post-training stage. Several other axes are deliberately out of scope. Extending the recipe to multi-step and agentic multimodal reasoning [6163] is open; we neither evaluate nor train for robustness, which carries its own substantial literature on robust and causal training [6468]; we do not consider inferencetime efficiency techniques such as visual-token pruning [69] that are orthogonal to the training recipe; we do not target conversational or social competence [70]; we do not touch the upstream perception stacks [71] that compact models ultimately depend on for embodied deployment; and our evaluation targets single-turn reasoning accuracy rather than complementary axes such as multi-sensor perception [72], multi-turn dialogue [73], or iterative self-refinement [74]. Combining ZPPO with these complementary directions is left to future work.

sections.title: Ethical Considerations
sections.content: ZPPO is post-training recipe that builds on the publicly released Qwen3.5 family (open-weight VLMs at 0.8B27B scale) [4]. We do not modify or filter the base models pretraining data; any social, demographic, linguistic, or modality biases present in the upstream pretraining of Qwen3.5 are inherited by the post-trained students. Our reward signal (rule-based exact-match grader plus LLM-as-a-judge for free-form questions) targets answer correctness on reasoning benchmarks, not safety, fairness, or harm reduction, so ZPPO is not substitute for dedicated safety alignment such as RLHF, Constitutional AI, or red-teaming. Practitioners deploying ZPPO-trained checkpoints should pair them with an upstream safety alignment stage and downstream content moderation appropriate to the deployment domain. Beyond inherited pretraining bias, dataset-level bias and spurious visuallinguistic correlations are distinct concern for multimodal models, addressed by debiasing and causal approaches [75, 76]; ZPPOs correctness-only reward neither introduces nor corrects such biases, so these directions remain complementary to our recipe. 12 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization

Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 36:4659546623, 2023. 24 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization A. Full Related Work This appendix expands the condensed discussion in Sec. 2 into the three full paragraphs that motivate ZPPO. Knowledge distillation for LLMs and VLMs. The dominant recipe for transferring stronger models competence to smaller one is knowledge distillation, which trains the student to imitate the teachers logits, hidden states, or sampled outputs [18]. In off-policy distillation, the target sequence is fixed ground-truth response [17, 19] or teacher rollout and the student is trained to match the teachers per-token logits, with recent variants adaptively scheduling student-generated outputs to balance the training-inference mismatch [21]. In on-policy distillation, the student instead samples its own response during training, that sequence is fed back into the teacher to obtain target logits, and the student is trained to match them [20, 2531]. Self-distillation removes the external teacher, instead using the student itself as the imitation target either via best-of-𝑁 rejection sampling on its own rollouts [43, 77], or by conditioning the same model on privileged information (e.g., demonstrations, verified traces, or environment feedback) to act as an in-context self-teacher [3235, 22]. All three families lean on the same training signal, logit distribution the student must match, and recent line of analyses shows that this signal turns brittle once the student is much smaller than the teacher: the student memorizes, mode-seeks, or loses general capability beyond the training data [2224]. ZPPO leaves this regime altogether. The teacher never enters the students policy gradient and shows up only as candidate inside the prompt. Reinforcement learning post-training for reasoning. RL post-training built on PPO [44] and group-relative variants such as GRPO [36] and DAPO [8] has, with rule-based rewards, pushed reasoning on math, science, and broad knowledge tasks [57, 9, 10, 38, 39, 37, 40, 41], and the same recipe extends to multimodal reasoning on imagequestion pairs [7884]. complementary line scales RL compute either by prolonging training steps or by broadening rollouts per prompt to extend exploration [38, 39], regulates entropy to prevent the policy from collapsing onto its current peaks [85, 86], and probes whether RL extends models reasoning frontier or merely sharpens what the model already knows [87]. However, these advances do not solve the small student failure mode on questions where the students every rollout still fails, the group advantage stays zero, and the question is silently filtered out as zero-advantage sample [8]. The natural fix replacing failed student rollout with teachers correct response inside the policy gradient breaks the on-policy assumption and induces severe policy drift; recent hybrid RL+distillation lines [45, 46] keep the teacher in the policy gradient and therefore inherit the same brittleness. ZPPO instead moves teacher knowledge from the gradient to the prompt: BCQ and NCQ inject teacher and self-generated candidates as reformulated prompts on those same hard questions, so every response token that enters the policy gradient is student-generated (on-policy at the response level). Prompt replay, curriculum, and hint scaffolding for RL. growing body of work reintroduces previously seen questions or rollouts in the spirit of classical experience replay [47, 48, 8890], or arranges questions by difficulty from easy to hard [49, 91]. Both lines re-prioritize hard questions but neither provides the guidance that distinguishes Vygotskys zone of proximal development [42] from mere difficulty ordering. complementary line scaffolds the student through difficult problems by injecting hints: prefix-based methods seed partial reasoning trace into the response so the rollout is part teacher and the policy gradient is no longer strictly on-policy [50, 51, 92], while prompt-injected hints add pre-authored guidance to the prompt only, keeping the response on-policy and optionally correcting the resulting off-policy bias by importance sampling [52, 93]. 25 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization ZPPO is also prompt-side method on the same hard questions, but its in-prompt content is BCQ and NCQ candidates assembled at training time from the current rollout batch not pre-authored hint text so candidates change dynamically as fresh rollouts are sampled throughout training, with the prompt replay buffer existing solely to amplify these reformulated prompts on questions inside the students current zone of proximal development. Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization B. Training Dataset and Benchmarks B.1. ZPPO-77K training corpus ZPPO-77K, the multimodal RL training corpus used throughout the paper, contains 77K (𝑥image, 𝑥text, 𝑎) triples where 𝑥image is the input image, 𝑥text is the text question, and 𝑎 is the short gold answer used as the rule-based reward target assembled from two publicly released sources: zlab-princeton/Vero-600k [94]1: 34 sub-datasets covering STEM (math, science, geometry diagrams), chart/OCR (chart, table, diagram, infographic), knowledge/recognition (general VQA), and counting/grounding/search. OpenDataArena/MMFineReason-SFT-586K [95]2: 586K-sample chain-of-thought VLM corpus annotated with per-example pass_rate computed by Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking (the smaller checkpoint released alongside the 235B-distilled corpus). The dataset name retains the 235B teacher tag because the corpus is distilled from the 235B model; the pass_rate column itself, which we use for our hard-example filter below, is the 4B models empirical success rate on each prompt. We split sub-datasets into two tiers by their relevance to challenging multimodal reasoning: Tier 1 (caps at 2,800 samples per sub-dataset): STEM and Chart/OCR sub-datasets that are directly aligned with the reasoning content the student is asked to learn (ai2d [96], geo170k [97], geomverse [98], geoqa_plus [99], mmk12 [100], cosyn_math/chart/diagram/table [101], wemath [102], tqa [103], visualwebinstruct [104], chartqa [105], arxivqa [106]; on the MMFineReason side, MMR1 [107], Euclid [108], Geo170K, Geo3K/Geometry3K [109], mm-openr1, WeMath, VisualWebInstruct, BMMR [110], TQA, AI2D, ScienceQA [111], ViRL39K [112]). Tier 2 (caps at 1,400 samples per sub-dataset): auxiliary VQA, knowledge, and counting/grounding sub-datasets (pathvqa [113], vqarad [114], raven [115], evochart [116], infographic_vqa [117], aokvqa [118], gqa [119], iconqa [120], vqav2 [121], visual7w [122], tallyqa [123], pixelreasoner [124], multihop, pixmo [125], visual_probe; MMFineReason Raven, VisualSphinx [126], PuzzleQA [127], LLaVA-CoT [128], Zebra-CoT [129]). Cross-source duplicates (e.g. Veros stem-ai2d_merged and MMFineReasons AI2D) are merged via canonical-name map and the priority-1 source (Vero) wins. Within MMFineReason we additionally drop every example whose Qwen3-VL-4B-Thinking pass_rate> 0.5 i.e. questions the 4B annotator solves more often than not so that the corpus is biased toward genuinely hard examples. Per-sample filters: answer length 512 characters, image 100 pixels in both dimensions (i.e., min(ℎ, 𝑤) 100). B.2. Evaluation benchmarks The 31-benchmark evaluation suite reported in the main paper consists of 16 VLM benchmarks, 10 LLM benchmarks, and 5 Video benchmarks. Tab. 5, Tab. 6, and Tab. 7 list the abbreviation used throughout the main-paper tables, the full benchmark name, the scoring protocol, and the HuggingFace data source. In the scoring column, Exact+Judge first applies exact/rule matching and falls back to the shared LLM-as-a-judge only on unresolved rows; Rule+Judge uses benchmark-specific deterministic parser before the same fallback; Det. uses deterministic official metric with no LLM-as-a-judge; and BenchJudge uses benchmark-specific official judge prompt. 1https://huggingface.co/datasets/zlab-princeton/Vero-600k 2https://huggingface.co/datasets/OpenDataArena/MMFineReason-SFT-586K-Qwen3-VL-235B-Thinking 27 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization

Training and evaluation deliberately use different decoding settings. Training-time student and teacher rollouts (Appendix D.1, Tab. 8) sample at temperature=1.0, top-p=1.0 with no top-𝑘/penalty terms, so the policy gradient sees high-entropy distribution and explores. Evaluation, by contrast, uses single more deterministic configuration shared across every benchmark and every model checkpoint temperature=0.6, top-p=0.95, top-k=20, min-p=0, presence-penalty=1.5, repetition-penalty=1.0, max-new-tokens=12288, max-model-len=262144, min-pixels=2563232, max-pixels=12803232 so that any difference between methods at the same student scale is attributable to training, not to evaluation hyperparameters. Prompt and judge templates. For every benchmark we strip all reasoningand answer-format directives from the upstream prompt (e.g. Think step by step or put your final answer within boxed{}) and rely on single shared RL closer that the student is also trained against  (Fig. 8)  : This guarantees that the answer-extraction format used at evaluation time is the same one the policy You FIRST think about the reasoning process as an internal monologue and then provide the final answer. The reasoning process MUST BE enclosed within <think> </think> tags. The final answer MUST BE put in boxed{}. Figure 8 RL closer enforcing the think/boxed answer format, applied identically at training and evaluation. gradient was optimized against. For benchmarks where exact-match parsing is unsafe, we use the shared LLM-as-judge only after deterministic parsing fails (e.g. math derivations and open-ended VLM/video questions), and use benchmark-specific judge prompts for official judge-based tasks (CharXiv, MM-Vet, HLE, MultiChallenge, and MMVU). The judge model itself is the same Qwen3.5-27B-FP8 used as the teacher in training (Tab. 8, Appendix G), running on dedicated sidecar pool at temperature=0.0, top-p=1.0, max-new-tokens=512, and parsing only strict JSON {"verdict": "correct" "wrong"} from the response (any malformed or out-of-vocabulary verdict is conservatively scored as 0, i.e. wrong). The same judge model, prompt, and fallback policy including the look-inside-reasoning fallback used when strict boxed{} cannot be parsed are applied identically to every method in the paper at every evaluation step, so any cross-method delta is attributable to method behaviour rather than to evaluation-protocol differences. The shared judge uses single Jinja2 template, reproduced verbatim in Fig. 9: The same judge configuration is used for every method in Tab. 1, Tab. 2, Tab. 3, Tab. 4, and the appendix tables. Benchmark-specific judge prompts. Five of our 31 benchmarks ship their own official judge prompt that we reproduce verbatim from the upstream evaluators so our reported numbers match the published leaderboards: MM-Vet, MultiChallenge, MMVU, HLE, and CharXiv (which uses two separate templates for its Reasoning and Descriptive question types). Figure 15 CharXiv Descriptive official judge prompt (7 rubric classes). Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization C. ZPPO algorithm Algorithm 1 summarizes one ZPPO training step. The key invariant is that every response used in the policy gradient is generated by the current student. The frozen teacher is queried only to provide prompt-side candidate text for BCQ (and to compress candidates under shared compression prompt and token cap), so teacher-generated tokens never enter the gradient as response tokens. ZPPO inherits its RL backbone from three lines of recent work and adds four novel components. Inherited from GRPO [36]: (G1) group-relative advantage formulation; (G2) PPO-style clipped surrogate objective. Inherited from DAPO [8]: (Da1) asymmetric clip-higher with (𝜖low, 𝜖high) = (0.20, 0.28); (Da2) token-level loss aggregation; (Da3) no KL penalty against reference policy. Inherited from REINFORCE++ [37]: (R1) two-step advantage estimator (Eqs. 23). Our recipe choice on top of REINFORCE++: (Z0) zero-advantage-group exclusion from the batch-statistics computation in Step 2, ablated in Sec. 4.5(ii) and derived in closed form in Eqs. 46. ZPPOs own contributions: (Z1) BCQ the anonymized binary-candidate prompt reformulation that turns hard question into discriminative one-of-two judgment between correct teacher trace and wrong student trace, both compressed by the same teacher under shared compression prompt and token cap to mitigate obvious surface cues in the discrimination; (Z2) NCQ the collectivenegative-candidate prompt reformulation that surfaces all of the students wrong rollouts on the same question, together with their parsed final answers, so the student must avoid the wrong rollouts it has just produced; (Z3) prompt replay buffer with hard-question admission, graduation by mean rollout accuracy, and FIFO eviction; (Z4) the super-additive combination of (Z3) with (Z1)+(Z2) that is the central empirical claim of the paper (Sec. 4.4). Algorithm 1 annotates each step with the tags above. few practical notes are worth pointing out. First, BCQ requires both correct teacher rollout and wrong student rollout to construct candidate pair; questions on which the teacher itself fails on every 𝑦T skip BCQ for that visit and only contribute NCQ (or, if all student rollouts succeed on the second try, neither). Second, candidate compression runs in parallel with Stage 1 scoring, so it does not stall the rollout loop. Third, the teacher rollouts {𝑦T} on replayed question are re-sampled on every visit the BCQ candidate seen by the student on visit 𝑘 is not the same as the one seen on visit 𝑘1, even when the underlying question is identical. This freshness is what allows the buffer to avoid degenerating into single fixed exemplar per hard question. Fourth, only the plain student rollouts on the original 𝑥 feed buffer admission and graduation in Stage 6: the BCQ/NCQ rollouts contribute to the gradient but are skipped when computing the per-question accuracy used to decide buffer membership, so question is judged mastered only when the student can solve it without candidate references. Fifth, the three groups (plain, BCQ, NCQ) on the same hard 𝑥 each have their own group identifier and are advantage-normalized independently; the plain group on 𝑥 and the reformulated groups on 𝑥BCQ, 𝑥NCQ are sampled from different prompt distributions and never share baseline. What counts as rollout step, gradient update, and token. Because 𝜌replay and 𝜌aug are both expressed as fraction of rollout_batch_size, the prompt counts seen by the gradient at each rollout step are additive rather than fractions of fixed budget. One rollout step contains: (i) 𝑋new new prompts drawn from 𝒟 (over the rollout-step budget in Tab. 8, 𝑋new alone consumes the entire ZPPO-77K corpus in single pass; 𝑋replay does not count against the dataset budget); (ii) 𝑋replay prompts drawn from ℬ on top of 𝑋new with 𝑋replay = 𝜌replay𝑋new; and (iii) at most 𝜌aug𝑋new BCQ/NCQ instances after the combined post-cap of Step 5. Baseline implementations This subsection describes the implementation of every baseline reported in Tab. 1, Tab. 2, Tab. 3, Tab. 13, and Tab. 4. All baselines share the same student initialization, the same dataset (ZPPO77K), the same evaluation pipeline, and the same rollout-side hyperparameters (rollout batch size, 𝐺S/𝐺T, sequence-length budget, optimizer, schedule). The gradient-side differs by loss family: RL methods (GRPO, GRPO, ZPPO, Hint, Prefix) split the per-step rollout batch into 𝐼 equal-sized mini-batches and apply 𝐼 gradient updates on the clipped surrogate (with PPO inner-epoch = 1 so no rollout token is re-iterated), while distillation methods (Off-Distill, On-Distill) apply single gradient update on the entire per-step rollout batch under the JSD imitation loss. Each rollout token therefore enters the gradient exactly once per rollout step under both families. Off-policy distillation. We instantiate the standard teacher-trajectory imitation paradigm of Hinton-style knowledge distillation [17] as adapted to sequence models by Sanh et al. [19], with two modifications motivated by the RL post-training setting: (O1) we filter the teachers trajectories by correctness using the same rule-based reward as ZPPO before they enter the imitation loss, instead of imitating the teacher unconditionally; (O2) we draw teacher trajectories online on every rollout step, with no precomputed pool, so that the buffer ablation in Tab. 3 is not confounded by one-time amortized teacher pre-compute. Concretely, on every rollout step, for every prompt 𝑥 in the rollout batch both new prompts 𝑥 𝑋new and (under the variant) replayed prompts 𝑥 𝑋replay we draw 𝐺T = 4 teacher rollouts {𝑦(𝑔) (𝑥)}𝐺T 𝑔=1 𝜋T(𝑥) online from the frozen teacher, grade them with the same rule-based reward as ZPPO, and keep only the correct subset {𝑦(+,𝑛) (𝑥)}𝑁𝑥 𝑛=1 (0 𝑁𝑥 𝐺T). The teacher samples for given 𝑥 on visit 𝑘 are statistically independent of those on visit 𝑘1 (whether or not 𝑥 is replayed). In parallel we draw 𝐺S = 8 student rollouts {𝑦(𝑔) (𝑥)} that are used for hard-prompt bookkeeping (and, under , for buffer admission/graduation), but the student rollouts never enter the imitation gradient. When 𝑁𝑥 > 0, we minimize the average over the 𝑁𝑥 correct teacher targets of the per-token JensenShannon divergence (JSD) between 𝜋𝜃(𝑥, 𝑦(+,𝑛) ) and the teachers per-token distribution at the same prefix [17, 19, 21] this is the standard sequence-distillation imitation loss specialized to the teachers correct trajectories; when 𝑁𝑥 = 0 (the teacher fails on 𝑥 at this step), the question contributes no imitation loss. Optimization shares the same AdamW configuration as ZPPO. The variant additionally maintains the prompt replay buffer ([O3]: our extension) with identical admission/graduation/eviction policies as ZPPO; because both new and replayed prompts re-draw teacher rollouts every step, Off-Distill matches ZPPOs teacher-freshness symmetrically on every visit. Compared to hypothetical offline-pool variant that caches the first batch of 𝐺T teacher rollouts per 𝑥 and reuses them on every revisit, the fully online setup we adopt costs proportionally more teacher-side compute (linear in the number of rollout steps prompt participates in), but it removes the cache-staleness confound when interpreting the buffer ablation in Tab. 3 any gain ZPPO shows over Off-Distill cannot be attributed to ZPPO seeing fresher teacher samples on the same hard prompt. On-policy distillation. We follow the on-policy distillation paradigm of Agarwal et al. [25], with per-token JensenShannon divergence (JSD) between student and teacher as the imitation loss. Specifically: (P1) the student samples its own trajectories rather than imitating teacher trajectories (the defining property of on-policy distillation); (P2) the frozen teacher rescores the students trajectories to produce per-token target distributions; (P3) the loss is the per-token JSD between student and teacher at the same prefix. Our only setup-specific extension is the optional buffer ([O4]) for the variant, which is the same prompt replay buffer used by ZPPO and Off-Distill. GRPO and GRPO. GRPO inherits three families of components from the recent RL posttraining literature, and our setup adds three minor changes. Adopted from GRPO [36]: (G1) the group-relative advantage formulation that draws 𝐺S rollouts per prompt and centers their rewards within the per-prompt group; (G2) the PPO-style clipped surrogate objective on the centered advantages. Adopted from DAPO [8]: (Da1) asymmetric clip-higher with (𝜖low, 𝜖high) = (0.20, 0.28); (Da2) token-level loss aggregation rather than sequence-level; (Da3) no KL penalty against reference policy (kl_coef= 0). Adopted from REINFORCE++ [37]: (R1) the two-step advantage estimator (within-group centering in Step 1 and across-group batch normalization in Step 2; see Eqs. 23). Our setup-specific recipe choices on top: (OG1) lower inner-iteration count 𝐼 = 4 instead of the standard 𝐼 = 16 (Sec. 4.5); (OG2) optional prompt replay buffer in the variant; (OG3) exclusion of zero-advantage (all-correct or all-wrong) groups from the batch-statistics computation in Step 2 of the two-step estimator (Eqs. 23; ablated in Sec. 4.5(ii)). GRPO is identical to ZPPO with the BCQ, NCQ, and replay-buffer branches all disabled; GRPO adds the buffer alone (𝜌replay = 0.25, same admission/graduation/eviction policy as ZPPO). (ii) GRPO, On-Distill, Off-Distill add the prompt replay buffer on top of their base method; the buffer contributes small additional overhead coming from buffer admission/graduation bookkeeping plus the longer responses that hard prompts elicit. Buffer dynamics across student scales This subsection complements Sec. 4.3 with two views of how the prompt replay buffer behaves over training at every student scale, all measured on ZPPO runs with the hyperparameters listed in Tab. 8 (Appendix D). Figure 20 Replay-buffer composition by entry rollout accuracy at admission, over training, for ZPPO at every student scale. Each panel stacks the four admission bins so the total band height is the buffer occupancy. (1) The buffer saturates earlier at small scales. In Fig. 20 the buffer hits its capacity ceiling within 70 steps at 0.8B and within 100 steps at 2B; at 4B and 9B it only approaches the cap toward step 200. The reason is simple. Weak students fail more questions, so admission outruns graduation and the buffer fills almost immediately, whereas stronger students graduate at close to the admission rate and fill it more slowly. Either way, every scale ends training near capacity, which is the trend Fig. 5b already hinted at in the main paper. Figure 21 (left) Total number of graduations per scale, broken down by entry rollout accuracy at admission (0%, 12.5%, 25%, 37.5%). (right) Per-scale composition of graduates. Higher bars on the leftmost colors at larger scales mean ZPPO graduates larger absolute number of the hardest entries (admitted at 0%) at larger scales, not that hardest entries become smaller share of the buffer. (2) Even the hardest admission bin graduates, with the absolute count growing through 4B and the relative share rising monotonically up to 4B before saturating. Fig. 21 (left) shows that the count of graduations from the 0% admission bin (the bin where ordinary RL has zero gradient signal because every rollout fails) follows 293 432 562 514 across 0.8/2/4/9B, peaking Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization at 4B and dipping slightly at 9B as the saturated student needs fewer hard-question graduations to begin with. The 0% share of the per-scale graduate composition (right panel) tracks the same shape, climbing 13% 16% 21% and then holding flat at 21% from 4B to 9B. In short, BCQ and NCQ supply usable learning signal on exactly the questions where ordinary RL stalls, which is the mechanism the main paper invokes for Fig. 4. F.2. ZPPO vs. GRPO training dynamics at 2B This subsection isolates the source of ZPPOs advantage over GRPO at 2B by tracking the optimization-side and buffer-side dynamics of the two runs side-by-side. Figure 22 Training dynamics of Qwen3.5-2B + ZPPO vs. Qwen3.5-2B + GRPO. Left: student rollout accuracy. Center: fraction of student rollouts whose group-relative advantage is positive. Right: actor Shannon entropy of the policy distribution. (1) ZPPO sustains higher fraction of positive advantages. Fig. 22 (center) shows the fraction of student rollouts with positive group-relative advantage staying 3 pp above GRPO throughout training. The mechanism is direct: BCQ turns an all-wrong group (zero gradient signal) into mixed group as soon as one BCQ rollout is correct, and NCQ exposes the diversity inside an all-wrong group once at least two distinct failure modes appear. ZPPO therefore earns positive advantages on subset of groups that would give none under GRPO. (2) The student stays exploratory for longer. Fig. 22 (right) shows actor entropy under ZPPO climbing to 1.2 nats by step 200 vs. GRPOs 0.95, and rising rather than collapsing. We attribute this to the buffer: its continual re-admission of hard questions keeps slowing the policys concentration onto the easy distribution that GRPO converges toward. Figure 23 Replay-buffer fate of completed entries (graduated vs. FIFO-evicted) at 2B for ZPPO and GRPO. ZPPO almost doubles the graduation rate (26.6% vs. 14.3%) and proportionally cuts evictions, on the same buffer with the same admission/graduation thresholds. (3) ZPPO almost doubles the graduation rate at matched buffer policy. Fig. 23 compares the fate of every entry that has left the buffer by the end of training, at 2B and at matched buffer policy. ZPPO graduates 2,627 entries and evicts 7,251 (26.6% / 73.4%); GRPO graduates 1,440 and evicts 8,647 (14.3% / 85.7%). The buffer policy (admission threshold, graduation threshold, capacity, FIFO eviction rule) is identical in both runs; the admitted cohorts themselves differ per 54 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization run, since each run only admits questions on which its own plain rollouts fail. The comparison is therefore under matched buffer policy, what fraction of the admitted cohort ultimately graduates, not on the same set of questions, who graduates more. Read that way, the 1.9 graduation rate points squarely at the reformulation channel (BCQ/NCQ) as the thing that separates the two runs. Figure 24 (left two panels) Replay graduations partitioned by entry rollout accuracy at admission, for GRPO vs. ZPPO at 2B (counts and per-model composition). (right two panels) Per-bucket fate decomposition by number of re-sample attempts: ZPPO converts the improved (non-grad) (light green) bucket into graduated (dark green) much more aggressively than GRPO. (4) ZPPOs extra graduations concentrate in the hardest admission bins. Fig. 24 (left two panels) shows that under matched buffer policy, ZPPO produces 432 vs. 73 graduations from the 0% admission bin and 510 vs. 169 from the 12.5% bin (each run measured over its own admitted cohort, as in finding (3)). Hint and Prefix experimental setup and results This subsection details the implementation of the Hint and Prefix baselines reported in Tab. 4 and discusses the numerical pattern that the inline table summarizes. Hint follows the Guide-GRPO [93] / SEELE [52] family: the teacher generates an answer-free hint from correct teacher trace, the hint is appended to the prompt inside tag, and the student samples fully on-policy rollout from the hint-augmented prompt. Prefix follows BREAD [51] (with StepHint [50] as another member of the same family): correct teacher rollout is truncated to token-fraction prefix and forced as shared anchor for branched group of 𝐺S student continuations, with group-relative advantages computed over the branched group. For implementation simplicity we omit BREADs adaptive Episode Anchor Search (EAS) and fix the prefix fraction at 𝛼 = 0.4. Per-step algorithm annotations are in Appendix D.2 (Algorithms 5 and 6). Setup. Both baselines share the prompt replay buffer (admission, graduation, and capacity identical to ZPPO), the same student initialization, the same training dataset, and the same shared training pipeline as ZPPO. Per-method compute is bounded by the GRPOZPPO range reported in Tab. 9 (within the same training-budget convention), rather than listed as separate rows. Each baseline differs from ZPPO only in how it uses teacher-correct response on hard questions: Hint: single correct teacher rollout 𝑦(+) is converted by the teacher into an answer-free hint and appended to the prompt inside an explicit <hint> </hint> tag. The student then samples its rollout from this hint-augmented prompt; the rollout itself is fully student-generated and the gradient is on-policy at the response-token level (the prompt is augmented with teacher-derived hint text). (Sec. D.2, Hint baseline for the exact prompt template.) Prefix: single correct teacher rollout 𝑦(+) is truncated to its first 𝛼 = 0.4 token-fraction and forced as starting prefix to the students rollout; the student then samples its continuation autoregressively. The teacher-forced prefix tokens are masked from the policy-gradient summation following BREAD (E5 in Appendix D.2), so only the student-continuation tokens enter the gradient as targets. The remaining distinction from ZPPO is the content of the training signal: Prefix supplies only positive continuation cue (continue correct teacher trace), without paired wrong candidate to discriminate against (as BCQ provides) or the students own collective wrong rollouts to avoid (as NCQ provides). Neither baseline runs BCQ or NCQ. All other hyperparameters (Tab. 8) are identical to ZPPO. Result pattern. Tab. 4 surfaces three things at 2B that the appendix lets us interpret precisely. 57 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization (i) Hint helps moderate amount on VLM but plateaus. GRPO+Hint gains +1.2 pp on VLM over GRPO, but is still 0.4 pp below GRPO+BCQ and 1.6 pp below ZPPO. The student learns from teacher-side guidance, but the signal is one-sided: it never has to compare correct teacher candidate against its own wrong candidate, and it never sees its own wrong rollouts collected as negatives. The gain therefore saturates below BCQ and the full BCQ+NCQ recipe. (ii) Hint barely transfers to LLM or Video. GRPO+Hint comes in at 47.6 / 61.6 on LLM / Video, marginal versus GRPOs 47.3 / 61.9, because the hint acts as directional shortcut that the student copies without internalizing why the direction is correct, and exercises neither the contrastive discrimination nor the collective-negative avoidance that BCQ and NCQ supply. (iii) Prefix is worse on generalization. GRPO+Prefix loses 1.0 pp on LLM and 0.9 pp on Video relative to GRPO, while gaining only +0.3 pp on VLM. With teacher-prefix tokens masked from the policy loss following BREAD (E5), the gradient consumes only student-generated continuation tokens, so the gap is not gradient-side issue. The issue is what the training signal contains: Prefix supplies only single positive continuation cue per hard question correct teacher trace the student copies and extends as continuation shortcut without any paired wrong candidate to discriminate against (as BCQ provides) or any aggregation of the students own collective wrong rollouts to avoid (as NCQ provides). The gain on LLM and Video benchmarks therefore lags BCQ/NCQ, since those held-out suites stress discrimination and self-correction behaviors rather than continuation from known-good anchor. It all comes down to the gap between the best prompt-injection baseline (Hint, 47.6 / 60.4 / 61.6) and full ZPPO (50.4 / 62.0 / 63.2): +2.8 / + 1.6 / + 1.6 pp. We read that gap as the empirical price of any prompt-side method that drops (i) the discriminative two-candidate construction of BCQ, where one teacher-correct trace and one student-wrong trace are anonymized and shuffled with no correctness label, and (ii) the collective-negative aggregation of NCQ, where every wrong student rollout on the same question is surfaced together. Hint gives only an answer-free guidance signal with no negative side, and Prefix forces continuation from single teacher trace and surfaces no negatives. Neither comes close to BCQ or NCQ on either axis. F.6. Benchmark-selection robustness via cluster bootstrap We complement the training runs with benchmark-level cluster bootstrap covering every pairwise comparison reported in the main paper (Tabs. 1, 10, 2, 11, 3, 12, 13), not just the headline ZPPO GRPO delta. The procedure quantifies how robust each macro-level delta is to which exact benchmarks are included in the family. For each (scale, benchmark family) block we resample the benchmarks with replacement 10,000 times and re-compute the macro-average delta on each resample, then report the macro-average Δ, its 95% percentile CI, and the win-rate (number of benchmarks where ZPPO > baseline). The reported Δ is this benchmark-level macro-average (the mean of the per-benchmark deltas computed from unrounded scores), so it can differ by 0.1 pp from the Avg-row Δ printed in the main tables, which is the difference of the rounded column averages. Within each block we draw single shared benchmark resample per iteration that is reused across all columns and fix the RNG seed, so two columns that encode the identical comparison receive identical intervals and the cross-column deltas stay coherent. The Best non-ZPPO baseline is the strongest non-ZPPO row selected on the full sample and then bootstrapped under the same shared resample (it may be the untrained Base, e.g. on 4B/9B Video). Tab. 14 reports CIs for the main-result comparisons; Tab. 15 reports CIs for the ablation comparisons. Scope of the CIs. Because the resampling unit is the benchmark (not the training run or the stochastic decoding seed), these CIs quantify robustness to which exact benchmarks enter the macro58 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization average, not run-to-run training variance or per-decode sampling variance. Statements like the CI excludes zero below should be read as the macro-average Δ is robust to dropping or duplicating any single benchmark in the family, which is the appropriate stress test for benchmark-selection bias but is weaker than statistical significance claim against training-stochasticity null. Findings. (1) The buffer-isolated comparison ZPPO GRPO has 95% CI that excludes zero in every one of the twelve (scale, benchmark family) cells, with win-rate 8/10 in every cell and 9/10 in every cell except 0.8B LLM the pattern we would expect if the prompt-side recipe is what drives the headline gain once all other RL knobs are held fixed. (2) The two component-isolated comparisons ZPPO GRPO+BCQ (the NCQ marginal contribution) and ZPPO GRPO+NCQ (the BCQ marginal contribution) also have CIs that exclude zero in every cell, so neither reformulation channel can be dropped without giving up an empirically detectable share of the gain at the macro level under benchmark resampling. (3) The ZPPO Best-non-ZPPO comparison (the column-wise Δ rendered in the main and generalization tables, and the same per-cell quantity for the ablation tables even though those tables omit the Δ row) excludes zero everywhere except on Video at 4B and 9B in the main-result table, where the CIs are [0.24, +0.90] and [0.02, +0.86] pp respectively. The 0.8B students plain rollouts overlook cluster of straws on the right edge of pizza-meal photo and unanimously answer 0. Under BCQ the student is presented with one compressed candidate that localizes three straws on the right edge and another that concludes no straws visible; it commits to the positionally-grounded candidate on falsifiability grounds and recovers 3. Under NCQ where the teacher trace is removed and the student is shown eight copies of its own no straws answer with the instruction that 0 is wrong the 0.8B student fails to override its own consensus and repeats 0 on all eight rollouts. This is the qualitative shape of the match-neg behaviour we report aggregate at the 0.8B scale: the smallest student lacks the capacity to argue against its own collective negatives without teacher-positive anchor in the prompt, so the NCQ-only signal degenerates. Tab. 16 breaks the result down by judge correctness. We use the table to compare two readings: Under trivial answer-in-prompt match pattern, where the candidate format exposes which one is the teacher-correct trace, the student should pick the positive candidate on essentially every rollout and BCQ accuracy should approach 100%. Under genuine binary discrimination, where the anonymized, shuffled candidates carry no obvious surface tell after compression under the shared prompt and token cap, the student should match the positive candidate on the rollouts it gets right and match negative candidate on the rollouts it gets wrong, and BCQ accuracy should sit well below 100%. At every scale the audit favors the second reading over the first. NCQ audit. The NCQ prompt is constructed differently from the BCQ prompt: only the rewritten wrong student rollouts (student_neg_compressed) appear in the candidate block, together with the listed parsed final answers; the rewritten teacher rollout (teacher_pos_compressed) is logged for diagnostic purposes but never inserted into the NCQ prompt. We verified this directly on every NCQ prompt in the snapshots: 8/8 student_neg_compressed traces appear verbatim in the corresponding prompt, 0/4 teacher_pos_compressed traces appear in any prompt. The audit table for NCQ (Tab. 17) therefore changes meaning: match-neg is the rate at which the student repeats one of its own listed wrong answers (the NCQ failure mode the prompt asks it to avoid), and match-pos is the rate at which the students boxed string matches the rewritten teachers boxed string even though the teacher trace was not shown to it (the student arriving at the correct answer independently). The reading of the table is now: Under trivial avoidance pattern (e.g. the rewriter compressing the listed negatives so coarsely that the student can avoid them by emitting any non-listed string), NCQ accuracy should be high but match-pos should be near zero. Under rewriter-correctness pattern (the rewriter turning student_neg_compressed into teacher-style correct trace), the prompt would now contain the answer, match-neg should drop sharply across all scales, and NCQ accuracy should be high at every scale. The numbers are not consistent with either pattern. NCQ accuracy is intermediate and scaledependent (1.2% at 0.8B, 22%/35%/31% at 2/4/9B). match-neg at 0.8B is 82.7% i.e. when the smallest student is given its own collected wrong rollouts and told to avoid them, it repeats one of them as its final answer most of the time. Only as the student scales up does match-neg collapse (2.2% at 2B, 0.7%/0.2% at 4/9B), in lockstep with NCQ accuracy growing. This shape is what one would expect from student that gradually learns to discriminate against its own previously listed wrong answers; it is harder to reconcile with static avoidance pattern, where one would expect match-neg to be uniformly small from the first scale. Caveats on the audit. Three caveats apply to both tables. First, the comparison uses exact normalized boxed-string match, so student rollout that arrives at the correct answer through different surface text (e.g. versus the prose description spelled out by the rewritten teacher) is counted as other rather than as match. Second, the snapshots cover the convergence regime rather than the entire training trajectory; convergence-regime numbers do not by themselves rule out different match pattern earlier in training, although they are the regime in which any persistent match behaviour would have had the most time to be exploited. Third, on MCQ-style benchmarks with small answer spaces, NCQs collective negative listing can in principle help by elimination as well as by discrimination; the audit table cannot disentangle these two contributions, and the open-ended worked examples in Figs. 26, 27, 29, and 30 (free-form counting and chart-reading, all with open-ended numeric answer spaces) are where elimination is least likely to substitute for discrimination. 67 Zone of Proximal Policy Optimization

The teacherstudent gap is the narrowest of the three benchmark families (e.g. at 9B, every Video benchmark sits within few pp of the 27B teacher, with the largest residual on VMME at 5.9 pp), even though the student is trained as VLM and Video is purely generalization eval. G.3. Discussion Two observations on the 27B teacher link Tab. 18 and Tab. 19Tab. 21 back to the per-benchmark structure of ZPPOs gains. (1) The 27B teachers accuracy varies sharply across benchmarks. Even at 27B, the teacher is far from saturated on the hard math/reasoning slices: HLE (16.0), OCRZH (33.5), BabyV (30.4), and OCREN (55.7) all sit well below the macro-averages. BCQ candidates can therefore exist on hard benchmarks (HLE, BabyV, . . . ) because the teacher still occasionally solves what the student cannot, but the candidate pool is intrinsically thinner there than on saturated benchmarks (AI2D, MMLU-Rd, . . . ), and ZPPOs gains correspondingly correlate with how often the teacherstudent gap on benchmark is non-trivial but non-empty. (2) ZPPO-trained 9B student approaches the 27B teacher on several benchmarks. Per-benchmark, the 9B+ZPPO row of Tab. 19Tab. 21 sits within 1.0 pp of the 27B teacher on AIME25 (70.0 vs. 70.0), OCREN (56.7 vs. 55.7), InfoVQA, MultiCh, MVerse, MVista, MM-Vet, and WeMath. We do not interpret these proximate values as the student strictly surpassing the teacher: the per-run noise band on single ZPPO runs is comparable to the residual gaps. The largest remaining gaps (HLE, VBlind, VMME) line up with benchmarks where the 27B teacher itself is least saturated exactly the benchmarks on which BCQ has the least leverage because the teacher cannot supply many extra solved instances the student is missing. Putting the two together, the BCQ-pool argument can be read straight off Tab. 18Tab. 21. Under the 27B teacher we use throughout, ZPPOs per-benchmark gains follow wherever the teacherstudent capability gap is non-trivial but non-empty, and by 9B the student has closed in on the teacher across good part of the suite.
